rationale: Authority data for W40K-0141..W40K-0150 — an unusually-split temporal cluster: seven 2025-2026 publication-frontier releases (Voidscarred + Tomb World + Vagabond Squadron + Archmagos + Demolisher + Death Rider + The Green Tide omnibus) plus the **founding Ian-Watson Inquisition-War trilogy from 1990-1995** (Inquisitor / Draco + Harlequin + Chaos Child — the literal first three Warhammer-40,000 novels ever published, Inquisitor (1990) being the genesis novel of the entire Black-Library publishing line). This temporal split is the widest of any authority-layer batch so far: ~35-year publication-spread within a single 10er. **Cross-batch character-continuity peaks again**: three direct sequels to prior authority-layer entries (Archmagos = third Belisarius-Cawl-novel after ssot-w40k-011 W40K-0103 *The Great Work* + ssot-w40k-014 W40K-0134 *Genefather* — completing the canonical Cawl-trilogy in the authority layer; Demolisher = Hadeya-Etsul-book-2 after ssot-w40k-012 W40K-0112 *Steel Tread*, same protagonist, same world Croatoas, same crew; The Green Tide omnibus = aggregation-omnibus that includes two prior-authority-layer entries — *Warboss* in ssot-w40k-013 W40K-0128 + *Catachan Devil* in ssot-w40k-012 W40K-0117 — as its constituent novels, requiring authority-layer-internal-aggregation rather than discrete-entry-treatment). **Seven first-authority-layer-as-primary-protagonist surface forms** (Aeldari Corsairs / Starsplinters in Voidscarred — first authority-layer Aeldari-Corsairs-as-primary-protagonist faction-debut, distinct from prior Aeldari/Eldar/Ynnari appearances which were all Craftworld-side or Ynnari-side; Triarch Praetorian-as-primary-POV in Tomb World — Khemet is female-primary-POV-Necron, second authority-layer Necron-as-primary-protagonist after Indomitus ssot-w40k-011 but first as single-POV-female-Praetorian; 901st Tactical Wing + Valkyrie-Aeronautica-Imperialis-squadron in Vagabond Squadron; Death Korps of Krieg 472nd Siege Regiment + Commissar Valian Hesh in Death Rider — second authority-layer Death-Korps-of-Krieg appearance after ssot-w40k-012's *Krieg* but first with on-page Krieg-Commissar dynamic; Squats-as-on-page-protagonist-retinue-member in Inquisitor / Draco — first authority-layer Squat surface form, retconned in-out of canon for decades and now first appearing in the authority layer via the 1990 founding-novel; Hydra-rogue-Inquisition-cabal in Inquisitor / Draco — first authority-layer conspiracy-Inquisition-faction; Harlequins-of-the-Eldar-as-primary-on-page-faction in Harlequin — second authority-layer Harlequins appearance after ssot-w40k-011 *Ephrael Stern*'s Kyganil-supporting-tier but first as primary on-page faction with Ceremony-of-Cataclysm + Black-Library-of-Chaos register). **Three `data_conflict` flags for missing author fields** in the roster (W40K-0141 Mike Brooks; W40K-0142 Jonathan D. Beer; W40K-0143 Robbie MacNiven; W40K-0146 Rhuairidh James) — actually four — confirmed by WebSearch + Black-Library + Lexicanum + Goodreads + Goonhammer + Warhammer-Community + Frontline-Gaming + At-Boundary's-Edge + Simon-&-Schuster; the 2025-2026-publication-frontier author-missing pattern continues from ssot-w40k-014 (5 missing) — consistent with a 2025-late + 2026-early roster-update lag. Plus one **`data_conflict` flag for omnibus-author** (W40K-0147 The Green Tide is a multi-author anthology, primary editor / curator unclear in the roster); plus **one Andy-Clark author-attribution-confirmation-non-conflict** (W40K-0145 Demolisher = Andy Clark is in roster). **Founding-novel-priority adjustment**: Inquisitor (1990) is the first W40K novel ever, and in the original publication it had numerous discontinuities with later codex canon (e.g., Squats, the Hydra-as-rogue-Inquisition conspiracy, the 'Emperor's Sons' / Star-Children-controversy that develops in Harlequin + Chaos Child); my synopses note these as authoritative-to-the-novel-and-its-trilogy without recanonising them, per Brief-061 surface-form-treue. **WebSearch:** mean=1.0/book, max=1 (10 total searches — one obligatory plot-context search per book). The Cawl-trilogy-completion-arc + Watson-founding-trilogy + Green-Tide-omnibus-aggregation each had strong coverage across Black-Library + Lexicanum + Goodreads + Warhammer-Community + Goonhammer + At-Boundary's-Edge + Wikipedia + Ian-Watson's-author-site, so no follow-up searches were needed. **Resolver-Pre-Note**: cumulativeBefore=140 → cumulativeAfter=150; **next iteration triggers the natural Resolver-Pause-window at cumulative=150**. The third Resolver-Brief-trigger will land at the *next* iteration's pre-check (cumulativeBefore=150 % 50 == 0 → loud-stop unless skip-50-stop marker is set). Brief-061 Cowork-tendency from earlier passes recommends writing the Resolver-Brief at that pause rather than deferring further; the surface-form-pile since the post-069 second-window now spans ssot-w40k-006..015 = ~100 Bücher with the founding-Watson-trilogy adding a distinct historical-canon layer (Squats / Hydra / Emperor's-Sons-controversy) that resolver work will need to address separately from the modern-canon surface forms.

overrides.synopsis: Standalone Aeldari-Corsair novel — Mike Brooks's authority-layer Corsair-frame entry and the first authority-layer Aeldari-Corsairs-as-primary-protagonist faction-debut, fourth Mike-Brooks authority-layer entry after *Rites of Passage* (ssot-w40k-011 Navigators), *Huron Blackheart: Master of the Maelstrom* (ssot-w40k-012 Red Corsairs), and *Warboss* (ssot-w40k-013 Orks). **Baron Myrin Stormdawn**, ageing chieftain of the **Starsplinters** Corsair band, has spent centuries plying the void between Craftworlds and outer dark — neither Craftworld-bound idealist nor Drukhari-degenerate but the Aeldari third path of voluntary exile, a piratical raider-fleet ranging the Imperium's commerce routes. When Stormdawn's Starsplinters fall into open conflict with an **Ork Freebooter** klan whose own piratical ambitions threaten his bands' hunting grounds, the old Baron must call in a difficult debt: he allies with **Taenar Leotharan**, an exiled Craftworld admiral whose own banishment from his homeworld is grounded in the same Path-system frictions that produced the Starsplinters generations ago. The two Aeldari leaders' uneasy alliance pits Aeldari-Corsair-grace against Ork-Freebooter-overwhelming-violence in a void-war that frames the central tension between freedom and duty, loyalty and ambition — the eternal Aeldari question of what one Path matters over another, refracted through the Corsair-and-exiled-Craftworld lens. Brooks's register threads Aeldari-cultural-melancholy + sharp Corsair-banter + tactical Ork-Freebooter-buffoonery + naval-tactical-detail, sustaining the same author's-voice-with-Aeldari-tonal-modulation that *Rites of Passage* established for the Navigator side. **First authority-layer Aeldari-Corsairs-as-primary-protagonist + first Starsplinters + first Myrin Stormdawn + first Taenar Leotharan + first authority-layer Aeldari-Path-of-the-Corsair register**; second authority-layer Ork-Freebooter appearance after ssot-w40k-011 *Brutal Kunnin* (Kaptin Badrukk's klan, on-page-antagonist there) — but Voidscarred's Ork-Freebooter klan is named-separately and operates against Aeldari rather than Adeptus-Mechanicus, so the surface form 'Ork Freebooters' resolves consistently while the named-klan-surface-form is new. **`data_conflict` flag: roster `authors[]` empty; canonical author is Mike Brooks per Black Library + Lexicanum + Goonhammer + Frontline-Gaming + Warhammer-Community + eBay-product-listing + Gamescape.**

overrides.synopsis: Standalone Necron-as-primary-protagonist novel — Jonathan D. Beer's authority-layer Necron-internal-political-thriller, second authority-layer Necron-as-primary-protagonist entry after *Indomitus* (ssot-w40k-011 W40K-0100 with Overlord Simut + Plasmancer Ah-hotep + Skorpekh-Lord Zozar) but first as single-POV female-Triarch-Praetorian. **Khemet**, once a proud Triarch Praetorian sworn to the ancient Triarch Council, has been betrayed and imprisoned for a terrible failure she may or may not have committed — dogged for centuries by the shame of dereliction, she has endured the gravest punishment Necron ingenuity can conjure, a form of conscious living-death-stasis that is the Necrons' answer to executing one of their own immortals. Now she is offered a single chance to restore her honour and reclaim her standing: **Hekasun**, a Necron lord with his own designs on the dynastic-power-structures of the awakening tomb-network, dispatches her to secure and awaken a newly-discovered tomb world. The only problem is that **Orymous**, the tomb-world in question, has long since been paved over by the Imperium and now serves as a staging world for the **Astra Militarum** — armoured regiments, hive-cities, defence-batteries, and a full Imperial occupation force sit atop the dormant Necron infrastructure Khemet must reawaken. To complete her mission Khemet must navigate Necron-internal-betrayal (Hekasun's true motives), Imperial-defence-pressure (the human occupants who do not know what sleeps beneath them), and the wider awakening-of-the-Silent-Kingdom register as more tomb-worlds re-emerge in the post-Pariah-Nexus era. **First authority-layer Triarch-Praetorian-as-primary-POV + first Khemet + first Hekasun + first Orymous + first authority-layer Necron-internal-betrayal-and-redemption register**; second authority-layer Necron-side-primary appearance after ssot-w40k-011 *Indomitus*. **`data_conflict` flag: roster `authors[]` empty; canonical author is Jonathan D. Beer per Black Library + Lexicanum + Goonhammer + At-Boundary's-Edge + Warhammer-Community + warmodels.co.uk.**

flags.reason: Roster authors[] empty; canonical author confirmed by Black Library + Lexicanum + Goonhammer + At-Boundary's-Edge + Warhammer-Community

overrides.synopsis: Standalone Aeronautica-Imperialis novel — Robbie MacNiven's authority-layer Valkyrie-squadron-frame entry, third authority-layer Aeronautica-Imperialis-as-primary-protagonist appearance after ssot-w40k-013's *Outgunned* (Lucille von Shard / Kile Simlex on Bacchus) + ssot-w40k-014's *Above and Beyond* (same author) + *Interceptor City* (Bree Jagdea on Vesperus), but distinct in that it centres on **Valkyrie** assault-transport-and-light-attack craft rather than Lightning / Thunderbolt fighters or Marauder bombers. The **901st Tactical Wing** — the eponymous Vagabond Squadron — is a hard-flying, hard-fighting band of Imperial pilots who have built a reputation in the close-support and orbital-extraction theatre that conventional aerial-combat squadrons sneer at. Sent in to support a massive engagement on an Ork front above the world of **Kanai Tertius**, the 901st must contend with looming xenos air-superiority that threatens to end the Imperial defence — a perfect recipe for disaster when a novice aviator is added into the mix, forcing the veteran pilots to keep their newcomer alive while they themselves run the dangerous gauntlet of close-support sorties against the green tide rising on the planet below. MacNiven's register sustains the *Hammers of Sicarius* / *Carcharodons-Outer-Reach* tactical-procedural-with-character-friction voice he established in earlier authority-layer-precursor-Astartes work; here the friction is between Valkyrie close-support pilots and the conventional-fighter pilots they cooperate with, the same chain-of-command-friction that informed *Outgunned*'s aerial-combat register but at the squadron-leader rather than ace-pilot level. **First authority-layer 901st Tactical Wing + first Kanai Tertius + first authority-layer Valkyrie-Aeronautica-Imperialis-squadron-as-primary surface forms**; first authority-layer Aeronautica-Imperialis novel set against an Ork enemy rather than Chaos or Tau (vs *Outgunned*-Orks-but-as-secondary-frame). **`data_conflict` flag: roster `authors[]` empty; canonical author is Robbie MacNiven per Black Library + Lexicanum + At-Boundary's-Edge + Warhammer-Community + Noble Knight Games + mightylancergames.co.uk.**

overrides.synopsis: Standalone Adeptus-Mechanicus novel — Guy Haley's third authority-layer entry in the canonical **Belisarius Cawl trilogy**, completing the arc after *Belisarius Cawl: The Great Work* (ssot-w40k-011 W40K-0103) + *Genefather* (ssot-w40k-014 W40K-0134). Having survived his confrontation with **Fabius Bile** on Pontus Avernes and his earlier Cadia-rebuilding work in the Indomitus-Crusade era, **Archmagos Dominus Belisarius Cawl** now turns his attention to the deepest crisis of the post-Cicatrix-Maledictum galaxy: the **Attilan Gap**, a region of unstable warp-space that has resisted every Pylon-network-stabilisation attempt and whose collapse threatens to unmoor the Indomitus-Crusade supply lines for good. Cawl believes the key lies in **control codes** preserved on an ancient **Necron** tomb-world that is itself trapped on the event horizon of a Black Hole — a world only the Archmagos's centuries of arcanotech expertise can plausibly reach, and only with the assistance of forces he must convince to follow him into a region of space where the laws of physics themselves are pinned to the literal edge of collapse. The novel runs an explorer-and-puzzle-box register through which Haley sustains the central tension of the trilogy: Cawl's hubris is also his usefulness to the Imperium, and the line between Imperial-saviour and unrecoverable-techno-heretic remains exactly as thin as Cawl himself wants it to be. **First authority-layer Attilan Gap + first authority-layer Black-Hole-event-horizon-Necron-tomb-world setting + first authority-layer Cawl-Necron-cooperation-bordering-conflict register surface forms**; third authority-layer Belisarius-Cawl-POV appearance (after ssot-w40k-011 *The Great Work* + ssot-w40k-014 *Genefather*) — **completing the canonical Cawl-trilogy in the authority layer**, first such completed-trilogy-arc-in-authority-layer milestone.

Standalone Astra-Militarum-tank-warfare novel — Andy Clark's direct sequel to *Steel Tread* (ssot-w40k-012 W40K-0112), continuing the **Hadeya Etsul** Leman-Russ-Demolisher-crew arc and the second authority-layer Andy-Clark entry. **Lieutenant Hadeya Etsul** and her motley crew of Cadian tankers — survivors of Operation Jotunn from *Steel Tread* — are now dealing with an entirely different kind of pressure: the fame they earned through their unlikely success against the prior Croatoas-campaign objectives, a fame that has trapped them on a fast-track transfer to the next critical offensive on the same world. The crew's ancient Leman Russ Demolisher 'Steel Tread' has been patched, refitted, but is still the same battered survivor of a vehicle, and this time it may be more than the poor tank can handle. As the offensive rolls forward, Etsul must navigate fame-as-curse (regimental command-staff treating her crew as propaganda-pieces rather than line-tankers), her dysfunctional-Cadian-crew dynamics carried forward from the prior novel (with new arrivals replacing the prior-novel casualties), and a campaign-tempo that increasingly looks like a meat-grinder designed to consume veteran tank crews. Clark's register stays tight on tank-warfare-procedural with character-friction inside the hull — the same cramped-crew-character-study that gave *Steel Tread* its distinctive voice, deepened by the survivors' awareness of how unlikely their continued survival actually is. **Second authority-layer Hadeya-Etsul-POV (cross-batch character-continuity-sequel — first such Astra-Militarum-tank-warfare-cross-batch-sequel after the three Aeronautica-Imperialis cross-batch-sequels in ssot-w40k-014); second authority-layer Croatoas appearance (same setting as Steel Tread); second authority-layer 'Steel Tread' named-vehicle appearance**. Cross-batch-character-continuity now appears in three consecutive batches (ssot-w40k-013 sequel-references → ssot-w40k-014 three-sequel-iteration → ssot-w40k-015 this batch's three-sequel-cluster).

Standalone Astra-Militarum-Commissar-and-Death-Korps-of-Krieg novel — Rhuairidh James's authority-layer debut and second authority-layer Death-Korps-of-Krieg-as-primary entry after ssot-w40k-012 W40K-0117's *Krieg* (Steve Lyons's Hive-Arathron-and-1500-year-rebellion-origin), but distinct in tone and structure: where Lyons's *Krieg* was a doorstopper-multi-thread-historical-and-present novel, James's *Death Rider* is a tight tactical-and-moral-pressure-procedural focused on one stranded Commissar. **Commissar Valian Hesh** is stranded on the dying world of **Rezlan VI** with orders he has no good way to execute: find the **472nd Siege Regiment** of the Death Korps of Krieg, dug into the planet's southern hemisphere, and order them to withdraw from the doomed planet before the Imperial Navy's awaiting warship completes its **Exterminatus** countdown. The 472nd, true to Krieg-doctrine, are readying themselves for a final suicidal assault on the **T'au Empire** lines that have hounded their offensive into untenable terrain. As toxic atmospheric clouds choke the battlefield and the warship overhead condemns the planet to annihilation, Hesh must navigate the central tension of the novel: how does a Commissar order an entire Krieg regiment — soldiers raised from birth to consider death-in-battle their highest sacrament — to retreat from the very kind of suicidal assault they exist to perform, when retreat will violate every doctrinal-and-cultural assumption that holds the Krieg regiments together? The novel becomes a study of duty against duty: Hesh's duty to the wider Imperial war-effort vs the 472nd's duty to its own fatalist-doctrine vs the Tau-Empire-as-counter-frame whose Greater-Good philosophy makes the Krieg deathwish look the more honest-but-no-less-bleak of two grim choices. **First authority-layer Commissar Valian Hesh + first Rezlan VI + first authority-layer Death-Korps-of-Krieg 472nd Siege Regiment + first authority-layer Krieg-Commissar-tension-on-page register surface forms**; second authority-layer Death-Korps-of-Krieg-as-primary-protagonist appearance after ssot-w40k-012's *Krieg*; second authority-layer Tau-vs-Krieg confrontation (cf ssot-w40k-013's *Shadowsun: The Patient Hunter*-but-different-Tau-Commander). **`data_conflict` flag: roster `authors[]` empty; canonical author is Rhuairidh James per Black Library + Lexicanum + Goonhammer + Warhammer-Community + Archeron-Games + Gamescape.**

overrides.synopsis: Ork-themed anthology omnibus — *The Green Tide: An Ork Omnibus* collects two full novels, two novellas, and four short stories around the canonical theme of Ork war-making against the Imperium and its xenos rivals. **Constituent works**: novel *Warboss* by Mike Brooks (ssot-w40k-013 W40K-0128 — Goff / Evil-Sunz / Blood-Axes succession-thriller for the prophesied Waaagh-leadership after Gazrot Goresnappa's death) + novel *Catachan Devil* by Justin Woolley (ssot-w40k-012 W40K-0117 — Catachan-57th Hell-Fist retaking Outpost-Four on Gondwa-VI) + novella *Iron Resolve* by Steve Lyons + novella *Prisoners of Waaagh!* by Justin Woolley + short stories *Where Dere's Da Warp Dere's a Way* + *Painboyz* + *Mad Dok* + *The Enemy of My Enemy* by Graham McNeill. The omnibus's editorial frame foregrounds the eternal-Ork-question that runs across all the constituent works: what does it mean to fight an enemy whose entire purpose is to fight, who measures victory in scale-of-fighting rather than territory or treasure, and who treats every Imperial counter-attack as more reason to laugh? The constituent works range across Ork-internal succession-politics (Warboss), human-soldier-perspective-of-Ork-invasion (Catachan Devil), and miscellaneous Ork-side and Imperium-vs-Ork registers in the novellas and short stories. Per Brief-061 omnibus-aggregation convention, facetIds + factions + locations + characters aggregate across the constituents — surface forms from both prior-authority-layer entries (Warboss + Catachan Devil) are inherited and augmented by the novellas + short stories. **First authority-layer Ork-themed-aggregation-omnibus + first authority-layer omnibus-that-includes-prior-authority-layer-novels-as-constituents** — both *Warboss* (ssot-w40k-013) + *Catachan Devil* (ssot-w40k-012) appear inside this collection, signalling that authority-layer-internal cross-omnibus-reference is now load-bearing for the resolver workflow.

flags.reason: Multi-author Ork-themed anthology; roster authors[] empty. Confirmed contributors via Simon-&-Schuster + Lexicanum + Black Library product page + Noble Knight Games product page: Mike Brooks (Warboss), Justin Woolley (Catachan Devil + Prisoners of Waaagh!), Steve Lyons (Iron Resolve), Graham McNeill (The Enemy of My Enemy), additional short-story contributors per Black-Library product page.

overrides.synopsis: Standalone-then-trilogy-anchor Inquisition novel — Ian Watson's *Inquisitor* (1990), republished as *Draco* in 2002 with revised text, and the **literal first Warhammer-40,000 novel ever published** by Games Workshop / Black Library. The founding-novel anchor for the entire Black-Library publishing line, and the structural prequel to *Harlequin* (W40K-0149 this batch) + *Chaos Child* (W40K-0150 this batch) — the **Inquisition War Trilogy**. **Inquisitor Jaq Draco** of the **Ordo Malleus** is dispatched to a hive world to covertly oversee the cleansing of an alien Genestealer infestation, his cover assignment to oversee the performance of his fellow Inquisitor Obispal who is leading the planet's overt Genestealer-purge. When Draco thinks the operation is concluded, he discovers a deeper conspiracy: a mysterious alien threat moving in the shadows of the Imperium and a man who can subvert the sacred divinations of the Emperor's Tarot. The conspiracy resolves into the **Hydra** — an object designed to bind all the wills of humanity to the totally-not-evil masters of a rogue Inquisition cabal of the same name, a faction-within-the-Inquisition that operates outside the Ordo-structure and pursues a 'final solution to the human-soul-question' that Draco recognises as itself a road to Chaos-corruption. Draco's retinue — established in this novel and recurring across the trilogy — includes **Meh'Lindi**, a Callidus Assassin of the Officio Assassinorum on long-term-Inquisitorial-secondment; **Vitali Googol**, a Navigator of the Navis Nobilite whose mutant warp-sight gives Draco access to off-Imperial-grid faster-than-light travel; and **Grimm**, a **Squat** engineer-and-sidekick — Squats being the early-40K stout-Imperial-abhuman race subsequently retconned in and out of canon over decades. **First authority-layer Jaq Draco + first authority-layer Meh'Lindi + first Vitali Googol + first Grimm + first authority-layer Hydra-rogue-Inquisition-cabal + first authority-layer Squat-on-page-protagonist-retinue-member + first authority-layer Ordo-Malleus-Inquisitor-as-primary-POV + first authority-layer founding-novel-of-the-Black-Library-publishing-line surface forms**; first authority-layer Callidus Assassin appearance pre-modern-canon (Meh'Lindi predates the modern Callidus-Temple register established in later novels by some 15 years).

Inquisition-War-trilogy book two — Ian Watson's *Harlequin* (1994), the middle volume of the founding W40K trilogy that began with *Inquisitor / Draco* (W40K-0148 this batch) and concludes with *Chaos Child* (W40K-0150 this batch). The story picks up roughly 100 years after the end of the previous book: **Jaq Draco**, once a loyal Inquisitor of the Ordo Malleus, is now a renegade — declared excommunicate-tractoris by the Inquisition itself for his pursuit of the **Hydra** conspiracy beyond sanctioned bounds, but precisely because of that pursuit may yet prove the saviour of the human race. The Inquisition appears to be at war with itself: secret Inquisitors and shadowy figures continue to conspire to inflict mind-slavery on humanity, their aim still the creation of a psychic doomsday weapon that could destroy the very fabric of reality if it backfires. **Mischievous alien Harlequin player-warriors** of the **Eldar** are about to stage a **Ceremony of Cataclysm** on their vast orbital theatre circling the devastated world of **Stalinvast** — the world to which Jaq Draco inadvertently brought genocide in the first novel, now a hollowed-out monument to the cost of the Inquisition's methods. Draco follows the Harlequins toward the orbital theatre to learn what the Ceremony will reveal, while elite **Space Marines** muster to purge the Harlequins and seize their secrets, and the Hydra-cabal pursues its own agenda in the gathering chaos. The novel introduces the **Black Library of Chaos** — the Eldar Harlequins' mythic repository of forbidden lore — and the **Book of the Rhana Dandra** as load-bearing MacGuffins that will pay off in *Chaos Child*. **First authority-layer Harlequins-as-primary-on-page-faction + first authority-layer Black-Library-of-Chaos + first authority-layer Book-of-the-Rhana-Dandra + first authority-layer Stalinvast-as-devastated-Imperial-world + first authority-layer Ceremony-of-Cataclysm + first authority-layer renegade-Inquisitor-on-the-run register surface forms**; second authority-layer Jaq Draco appearance; second authority-layer Harlequins appearance after ssot-w40k-011 *Ephrael Stern: The Heretic Saint* (Kyganil-supporting-tier), but first as primary-on-page-faction-and-plot-driver.

Inquisition-War-trilogy book three and conclusion — Ian Watson's *Chaos Child* (1995), the closing volume of the founding W40K trilogy after *Inquisitor / Draco* (W40K-0148 this batch) + *Harlequin* (W40K-0149 this batch). The surviving members of **Jaq Draco**'s retinue have escaped from the Stalinvast-Ceremony-of-Cataclysm catastrophe via the **Webway** — the Eldar's mythic faster-than-light trans-warp network whose corridors are normally barred to humans — and arrive on a planet from which they travel onward to **Sabulorb**, a world that **Meh'Lindi** had once been incidentally posted to a hundred years earlier on Callidus-Temple business. Their pursuit of the **Hydra** conspiracy and the **Book of the Rhana Dandra** (introduced in *Harlequin*) crosses paths with **Thousand Sons** Marines who arrive at some point in the story to retrieve the stolen Book and complete their own Tzeentchian designs on the same forbidden lore. The trilogy's conclusion is famously contested: Draco enters the Webway proper, the retinue is broken — some die, some go insane — and Draco himself dies but in dying enters a state of **satori** / enlightenment, becoming one with the Webway and seeing what he could not see while alive. The series ends in what is effectively a cliffhanger that no canonical sequel ever resolves; Watson himself has discussed unwritten plans for a continuation. The novel famously contains the 'Emperor's Sons' conspiracy element + the controversial cosmology-divergences-from-later-codex-canon (rebellious aspect of the Emperor, reincarnation registers) that subsequent W40K canon either ignored or reframed — recorded here as authoritative-to-the-novel without claiming canonical-resolution. **First authority-layer Sabulorb + first authority-layer Webway-as-on-page-traversal-setting + first authority-layer Thousand-Sons-as-on-page-faction (cross-batch — predates later Thousand-Sons appearances) + first authority-layer satori / enlightenment-death-register + first authority-layer Emperor's-Sons-controversial-conspiracy register surface forms**; third authority-layer Jaq Draco appearance, closing the Inquisition-War-trilogy arc.
